title: Middlewares
description: Built-in middleware and writing your own

blocks.text: Middleware is the backbone of Howl's request pipeline. Every app.use() call adds a function that can inspect, modify, or terminate a request before it reaches a route handler.

rows: Gzip/deflate for text, JSON, JS, SVG

blocks.text: Compresses responses with gzip or deflate based on the client's Accept-Encoding header. Skips binary content types and already-encoded responses.

import { compression } from "@hushkey/howl/middleware";
app.use(compression());
Place compression() early so it wraps all downstream responses including static files and API routes.
When many concurrent GET requests arrive for the same URL (thundering herd), only one handler runs. All others await and receive a clone of the first response.
Only unauthenticated requests are coalesced — any request with a Cookie or Authorization header gets its own handler execution to prevent state leakage.

import { coalesceRequests } from "@hushkey/howl/middleware";
app.use(coalesceRequests()); // place before route handlers

Sets a Content-Security-Policy header on every response. By default, csp() generates a fresh nonce per request and emits script-src 'nonce-…' 'strict-dynamic' 'self' — strict CSP works with no 'unsafe-inline' required. The same nonce is propagated to the bootloader <script> so it executes under the policy.
Options:

rows: Generate a per-request nonce. Set to false to fall back to script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'
Switch to Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only header (observe without enforcing)
If set, adds report-to / report-uri / Reporting-Endpoints header
Extra directives appended to the defaults

blocks.text: Test in report-only mode first: csp({ reportOnly: true, reportTo: "/api/csp-report" }) lets you collect violations without breaking the site.
Normalises every URL to either always or never have a trailing slash, redirecting mismatches with a 308 response.

The router itself is now also tolerant of trailing-slash mismatches: a request to /wissen/ matches a route registered as /wissen (and vice versa) without the middleware. The middleware is only needed when you want to canonicalise URLs for SEO/analytics or force a redirect.
When two routes are registered that differ only by trailing slash (e.g. /wissen and /wissen/), exact matches take priority — the fallback only kicks in when no exact match exists.
A middleware is any function that takes a Context and returns a Response. Call ctx.next() to pass control downstream.
Use ctx.partialRedirect() instead of ctx.redirect() in middleware guards — it preserves the SPA partial nav state so the browser doesn't do a full reload after the redirect.
Middleware can also be defined in files following the .middleware.ts convention. These apply to all routes within their directory segment.
